enrichment.short_description: Open-source, OpenTelemetry-native observability platform providing APM, logs, metrics and traces in one tool, positioned as an open-source alternative to Datadog and New Relic.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (free self-hosted open source plus usage-based paid cloud)
enrichment.starting_price: $49/month (Cloud Teams plan); free for self-hosted Community edition
enrichment.seo_title: SigNoz Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SigNoz is an open-source, OpenTelemetry-native observability platform offering APM, logs, metrics and traces in one tool. Compare pricing, features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SigNoz is an open-source observability platform built natively on OpenTelemetry that unifies application performance monitoring (APM), log management, infrastructure metrics, and distributed tracing in a single tool. Founded in 2021 by Pranay Prateek and Ankit Nayan and backed by Y Combinator and investors including SignalFire, SigNoz was created as an open-source alternative to proprietary observability vendors such as Datadog and New Relic. The company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with an engineering presence in Bangalore, India. SigNoz can be self-hosted for free under an open-source license (Community Edition), giving engineering teams full data ownership and no per-seat licensing costs, or consumed as a managed SigNoz Cloud service that starts at 49 US dollars per month on the Teams plan, billed on usage of ingested logs and traces (0.30 US dollars per gigabyte) and metric samples (0.10 US dollars per million samples), with no charges based on user seats or number of hosts monitored. An Enterprise tier adds dedicated cloud or bring-your-own-cloud deployment, HIPAA and SOC 2 Type II compliance, dedicated support, and guided migration from other observability tools. A Startup Program offers 50 percent discounted Cloud pricing to eligible early-stage companies. SigNoz integrates directly with the OpenTelemetry standard, so teams instrumenting applications with OTel collectors and SDKs can send data to SigNoz without vendor-specific agents. It is commonly evaluated by engineering and DevOps teams looking to reduce observability costs while keeping flexibility to self-host or migrate away from closed-source APM vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is SigNoz
paragraphs: SigNoz is an open-source observability platform that combines application performance monitoring, log management, infrastructure metrics, and distributed tracing into a single product built natively on OpenTelemetry. It was created in 2021 by Pranay Prateek and Ankit Nayan and has since been backed by Y Combinator and investors such as SignalFire, positioning itself directly as an open-source alternative to Datadog and New Relic.

Because SigNoz is OpenTelemetry-native, engineering teams that have already instrumented their services with OTel collectors and SDKs can point that data at SigNoz without switching to a proprietary agent. Teams can either self-host the Community Edition for free or use SigNoz Cloud, a managed offering that removes the operational burden of running and scaling the observability backend.

SigNoz is aimed primarily at engineering, DevOps, and SRE teams that want the depth of a commercial APM tool without vendor lock-in or unpredictable per-seat pricing, and who value the ability to inspect, extend, or self-host the underlying code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of SigNoz
paragraphs: SigNoz brings together the three pillars of observability, metrics, logs, and traces, in one correlated interface, so engineers can move from a metric spike to the related traces and logs without switching tools.

The platform ships with pre-built dashboards for common services and infrastructure, customizable alerting across metrics, logs, and traces, and a query builder that lets teams explore telemetry data without writing raw query language by hand.

Because it is open-source, SigNoz can be deployed on-premises, in a private cloud, or via Docker and Kubernetes, giving security- and compliance-sensitive teams full control over where their observability data lives.

long_form_blocks.heading: SigNoz Pricing
paragraphs: SigNoz Community Edition is free and open-source, self-hosted, and includes the full observability feature set with no license fees, though teams are responsible for provisioning and operating the infrastructure themselves.

SigNoz Cloud starts on the Teams plan at 49 US dollars per month, which includes a usage allowance and unlimited teammates and hosts; usage beyond that allowance is billed at 0.30 US dollars per gigabyte of logs or traces ingested and 0.10 US dollars per million metric samples.

An Enterprise plan adds dedicated or bring-your-own-cloud deployment, HIPAA and SOC 2 Type II compliance, guided migration, and dedicated support, with custom pricing that typically starts around 4,000 US dollars per month; eligible early-stage startups can also apply for 50 percent off Cloud pricing through the SigNoz Startup Program.

features.title: Unified metrics, logs and traces
features.description: View and correlate application metrics, logs, and distributed traces in a single interface instead of switching between separate tools.

features.title: Native OpenTelemetry support
features.description: Ingests data directly from OpenTelemetry collectors and SDKs, so teams already using the OTel standard can adopt SigNoz without changing instrumentation.

features.title: Distributed tracing and flame graphs
features.description: Visualize request flows across microservices with trace waterfalls and flame graphs to pinpoint latency bottlenecks.

features.title: Custom dashboards and alerts
features.description: Build custom dashboards and configure alerting rules across metrics, logs, and traces with notifications sent to Slack, email, or webhooks.

features.title: Log management and query builder
features.description: Search, filter, and analyze high-volume log data with a visual query builder in addition to raw query support.

features.title: Exceptions and error tracking
features.description: Automatically capture and group application exceptions to help engineering teams triage and resolve errors faster.

features.title: Self-hosted or managed cloud deployment
features.description: Run SigNoz Community Edition on your own infrastructure for full data control, or use SigNoz Cloud for a fully managed backend.

features.title: Enterprise compliance
features.description: SigNoz Cloud offers SOC 2 Type II and HIPAA-compliant deployment options with dedicated support for regulated industries.

pros: Open-source with a free, full-featured self-hosted Community Edition
Native OpenTelemetry support avoids vendor-specific instrumentation lock-in
Cloud pricing is usage-based rather than charged per seat or per host
Unifies metrics, logs, and traces in one correlated interface
Active open-source community on GitHub with frequent releases
cons: Self-hosting requires engineering time to deploy, scale, and maintain the ClickHouse-based backend
Enterprise-tier pricing is not published and requires contacting sales
Smaller ecosystem of third-party integrations than long-established vendors like Datadog
Usage-based cloud billing can be harder to predict than flat per-seat pricing for high-volume workloads
As a newer company founded in 2021, long-term roadmap and support depth are still maturing compared to incumbents

faqs.question: Is SigNoz free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. SigNoz offers a free, open-source Community Edition that can be self-hosted with no license fees. SigNoz Cloud, the managed version, starts at 49 US dollars per month on usage-based pricing.

faqs.question: Is SigNoz an open-source Datadog alternative?
faqs.answer: Yes, SigNoz positions itself directly as an open-source alternative to Datadog and New Relic, offering APM, log management, and distributed tracing built on OpenTelemetry.

faqs.question: Who founded SigNoz?
faqs.answer: SigNoz was founded in 2021 by Pranay Prateek and Ankit Nayan and went through Y Combinator; the company is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Does SigNoz support OpenTelemetry?
faqs.answer: Yes, SigNoz is built natively on OpenTelemetry and ingests data directly from OTel collectors and SDKs without requiring a proprietary agent.

faqs.question: Can I self-host SigNoz?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community Edition can be self-hosted for free via Docker or Kubernetes, giving teams full control over their observability data.

faqs.question: How is SigNoz Cloud priced?
faqs.answer: SigNoz Cloud starts at 49 US dollars per month on the Teams plan and bills usage at 0.30 US dollars per gigabyte of logs or traces and 0.10 US dollars per million metric samples, with unlimited teammates and hosts.

faqs.question: Does SigNoz offer a startup discount?
faqs.answer: Yes, eligible early-stage startups under three years old, with fewer than 30 employees and under 6 million US dollars raised, can get 50 percent off SigNoz Cloud pricing through the Startup Program.

faqs.question: Is SigNoz suitable for regulated industries?
faqs.answer: SigNoz Cloud offers SOC 2 Type II and HIPAA-compliant deployment options on its Enterprise plan for organizations with stricter compliance requirements.

use_cases.title: Replacing a costly APM vendor
use_cases.description: Engineering teams migrate from expensive per-host or per-seat APM tools to SigNoz to cut observability spend while keeping OpenTelemetry-based instrumentation.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted observability for compliance-sensitive teams
use_cases.description: Organizations with data residency or security requirements self-host SigNoz Community Edition to keep telemetry data inside their own infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Unified debugging across microservices
use_cases.description: SRE and DevOps teams use SigNoz to correlate traces, logs, and metrics when diagnosing latency or error issues across distributed microservice architectures.

features: Self-hosted deployment via Docker or Kubernetes
Usage-based billing for logs, traces and metrics
SOC 2 Type II and HIPAA-eligible infrastructure
Dedicated Slack support and guided migration

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based e-signature platform for sending, signing and managing legally binding documents, acquired by Box in 2021.
enrichment.seo_title: SignRequest Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Box Integration
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SignRequest is an e-signature platform now owned by Box. See pricing, features, pros and cons, and how it compares to SignNow, SignWell and DocuSign.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SignRequest is a cloud-based electronic signature platform that lets individuals and businesses send, sign, and manage legally binding documents online. The company was founded in Amsterdam, the Netherlands, in 2014 and built a reputation as a simple, affordable e-signature tool for small and mid-sized businesses before being acquired by Box, Inc. in February 2021 for an aggregate purchase price of 55 million US dollars. Since the acquisition, SignRequest has operated as a wholly owned subsidiary of Box and its e-signature technology has been integrated into Box Sign, Box's native e-signature product; Box has stated it is no longer building new API integrations under the SignRequest name and points developers toward the Box Sign API instead. SignRequest still offers standalone plans directly, including a free tier for occasional signers sending up to a handful of documents per month, and paid Professional and Business tiers priced per user per month that add templates, team management, bulk sending, and integrations with tools such as Google Drive, Dropbox, and Salesforce. Typical SignRequest users are freelancers, small businesses, and teams that need straightforward, legally compliant e-signatures (eIDAS and ESIGN Act compliant) without the cost or complexity of larger enterprise contract lifecycle management suites. Because Box now owns the technology, prospective buyers evaluating SignRequest today should also consider Box Sign as the actively developed successor product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is SignRequest
paragraphs: SignRequest is an electronic signature platform founded in Amsterdam, the Netherlands, in 2014, built to make sending and signing legally binding documents fast and affordable for individuals, freelancers, and small teams.

In February 2021, Box, Inc. announced its intent to acquire SignRequest for an aggregate purchase price of 55 million US dollars, and the deal closed shortly after, making SignRequest a wholly owned subsidiary of Box. The acquisition gave Box native e-signature capabilities that now also power Box Sign.

SignRequest continues to operate as its own product with a self-serve pricing page, though Box has signaled that new API development is being consolidated into the Box Sign API rather than the legacy SignRequest API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of SignRequest
paragraphs: Users can upload a document, add signature fields, and send it for signature in a browser-based editor, with signers able to complete the process without creating an account.
SignRequest supports templates for frequently sent documents, bulk sending for sending the same document to many recipients at once, and team accounts with shared templates and centralized billing.

The platform includes audit trails, tamper-evident sealing, and compliance with major e-signature regulations such as the US ESIGN Act and the EU's eIDAS regulation, along with integrations for Google Drive, Dropbox, and Salesforce.

long_form_blocks.heading: SignRequest Pricing
paragraphs: SignRequest offers a free plan for individuals sending a limited number of documents per month, suitable for occasional or personal use.

Paid plans are priced per user per month: the Professional plan starts around 9 US dollars per user per month and the Business plan around 15 US dollars per user per month, with annual billing offering a discount versus monthly.

High-volume senders can add bulk-send document bundles for a per-document fee, and enterprise buyers are increasingly directed toward Box Sign, Box's broader e-signature product, for large-scale or API-driven needs.

features.title: Browser-based signing
features.description: Recipients can review and sign documents directly in the browser without creating a SignRequest account.

features.title: Reusable templates
features.description: Save frequently used documents as templates with pre-placed signature and data fields to speed up repeat sending.

features.title: Bulk send
features.description: Send the same document to a large list of recipients in one batch, useful for HR forms or agreements sent to many signers.

features.title: Audit trail and tamper-evident sealing
features.description: Every signed document includes a detailed audit trail and cryptographic sealing to prove authenticity and detect tampering.

features.title: eIDAS and ESIGN Act compliance
features.description: Signatures are designed to meet US and EU legal requirements for electronic signatures.

features.title: Team accounts and shared templates
features.description: Business plans allow multiple users to share templates, branding, and centralized billing under one team account.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Connects with Google Drive, Dropbox, and Salesforce to pull documents in and store signed files automatically.

features.title: Box ecosystem integration
features.description: As a Box subsidiary, SignRequest technology underpins Box Sign, giving Box customers native e-signature inside their existing content management workflows.

pros: Simple, easy-to-use interface with a low learning curve for senders and signers
Affordable per-user pricing compared to larger enterprise e-signature suites
Backed by Box, Inc., a well-established, publicly traded company
Free plan available for individuals with light signing needs
Legally compliant with ESIGN Act and eIDAS requirements
cons: Box has deprioritized new API integrations for SignRequest in favor of the Box Sign API
Fewer advanced workflow and contract-lifecycle features than larger platforms like DocuSign
Long-term product direction is uncertain as functionality consolidates into Box Sign
Limited standalone reporting and analytics compared to enterprise-grade competitors
Per-user pricing can add up for larger teams compared to flat-rate competitors

faqs.question: Is SignRequest still available in 2026?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignRequest continues to operate as a standalone e-signature product, though it is a wholly owned subsidiary of Box and its technology also powers Box Sign.

faqs.question: Who owns SignRequest?
faqs.answer: SignRequest has been owned by Box, Inc. since Box completed its acquisition of the company in 2021 for an aggregate purchase price of 55 million US dollars.

faqs.question: Where was SignRequest founded?
faqs.answer: SignRequest was founded in Amsterdam, the Netherlands, in 2014.

faqs.question: How much does SignRequest cost?
faqs.answer: SignRequest offers a free plan for light use, with paid plans starting around 9 US dollars per user per month for the Professional tier and around 15 US dollars per user per month for the Business tier.

faqs.question: Is SignRequest legally binding?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignRequest signatures are designed to comply with the US ESIGN Act and the EU eIDAS regulation, making them legally binding in most jurisdictions.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SignRequest and Box Sign?
faqs.answer: Box Sign is Box's native e-signature product built using SignRequest's underlying technology after the 2021 acquisition; Box has directed new API integrations toward Box Sign rather than the legacy SignRequest API.

faqs.question: Does SignRequest offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: SignRequest offers a free plan for occasional signers as well as a trial period on paid plans, typically around 14 days.

faqs.question: Can I send documents to multiple people with SignRequest?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignRequest supports both sequential and bulk sending, allowing a document to be sent to many recipients at once.

use_cases.title: Small business contract signing
use_cases.description: Small businesses and freelancers use SignRequest to send and collect signatures on contracts, proposals, and invoices without paper.

use_cases.title: HR onboarding documents
use_cases.description: HR teams use bulk send and templates to distribute offer letters and onboarding paperwork to new hires for e-signature.

use_cases.title: Box customers adding e-signature workflows
use_cases.description: Existing Box customers use SignRequest and Box Sign to add e-signature directly into their document management and content workflows.

enrichment.short_description: Enterprise digital signature server from Keyfactor (formerly PrimeKey) for automated code signing, document signing and timestamping.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open-source community edition free; enterprise custom quote
enrichment.seo_title: SignServer Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Enterprise Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SignServer is Keyfactor's digital signature server for code signing, document signing and timestamping. Compare community vs enterprise editions, pricing and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SignServer is a server-side digital signature platform used to automate code signing, document signing (PDF, XML, CMS, JAR), and RFC 3161 timestamping at scale, with private signing keys typically secured inside Hardware Security Modules (HSMs). SignServer was first released in 2005 by the Swedish company PrimeKey Solutions, and PrimeKey was acquired by Keyfactor in June 2021, making SignServer part of Keyfactor's PKI and digital trust product portfolio. Keyfactor is headquartered in Independence, Ohio, United States, and has roughly 500 to 600 employees globally. SignServer is distinct from consumer e-signature tools such as SignNow, SignRequest, or SignWell, it is infrastructure software aimed at security, DevOps, and PKI teams that need to sign software builds, containers, firmware, documents, or timestamps in an automated, auditable pipeline rather than collect handwritten signatures from individual people. SignServer ships in two editions: a free, open-source Community Edition licensed under LGPL v2.1 that provides the core signing engine and can be run from prebuilt binaries, containers, or source, and a commercial Enterprise Edition that adds guaranteed stability, vendor support, high-availability clustering, and deployment as a software appliance, hardware appliance, or managed cloud instance on AWS and Azure marketplaces. Enterprise and cloud-marketplace pricing is per-core and per-support-tier and is not publicly listed; buyers must contact Keyfactor sales for a quote, though 30-day free trials are available on the AWS and Azure marketplace listings. Typical use cases include code signing in CI/CD pipelines, PDF and document signing for regulated industries, and quantum-ready signing for organizations preparing for post-quantum cryptography requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is SignServer
paragraphs: SignServer is a server-side digital signature platform that automates the signing of code, documents, containers, firmware, and other digital artifacts, keeping private signing keys centralized and typically protected inside Hardware Security Modules (HSMs).

The product was first released in 2005 by PrimeKey Solutions, a Swedish PKI company, and has been developed for over two decades. Keyfactor, a US-based digital trust and PKI company headquartered in Independence, Ohio, acquired PrimeKey in June 2021 and now sponsors and sells SignServer as part of its Enterprise PKI product line.

Unlike consumer e-signature tools built for individuals to sign contracts by hand, SignServer is infrastructure software: it is embedded into CI/CD pipelines, release processes, and document-generation systems so that signing happens automatically, at scale, with a full audit trail.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of SignServer
paragraphs: SignServer supports a wide range of signature types, including JAR, CMS, OpenPGP, Debian package, PDF, XML, and plain code signing, as well as RFC 3161-compliant timestamping services.

The platform is designed for infinite horizontal scalability by running multiple SignServer instances behind a load balancer, and it integrates into CI/CD pipelines so builds and releases can be signed automatically as part of the deployment process.

SignServer includes quantum-ready code signing capabilities to help organizations prepare their signing infrastructure for post-quantum cryptography requirements, along with authentication controls and detailed audit logging for every signing operation.

long_form_blocks.heading: SignServer Pricing
paragraphs: The SignServer Community Edition is free and open-source under the LGPL v2.1 license, distributed as prebuilt binaries, Docker containers, or source code for organizations willing to self-manage the deployment.

SignServer Enterprise Edition is a paid, supported product available as a software appliance, a PrimeKey/Keyfactor hardware appliance, or a cloud edition on the AWS and Azure marketplaces, priced per CPU core of the underlying virtual host plus a chosen support tier.

Keyfactor does not publish list prices for Enterprise or multi-node deployments; organizations must contact Keyfactor sales directly for a quote, though 30-day free trials are available through the AWS and Azure marketplace listings.

features.title: Automated code signing
features.description: Signs software builds, JAR files, containers, and packages automatically as part of CI/CD pipelines rather than requiring manual, one-off signing.

features.title: Document and PDF signing
features.description: Applies digital signatures to PDF, XML, and CMS documents for regulated workflows that require verifiable, tamper-evident signing.

features.title: RFC 3161 timestamping
features.description: Provides trusted timestamping services so signed artifacts can be verified as valid at the time they were signed, even after certificates expire.

features.title: HSM-backed key protection
features.description: Private signing keys are centrally managed and can be protected inside Hardware Security Modules to prevent key exposure.

features.title: Horizontal scalability
features.description: Multiple SignServer instances can run behind a load balancer to handle high signing volumes with no single point of failure.

features.title: Quantum-ready signing
features.description: Supports post-quantum cryptography readiness for organizations preparing signing infrastructure for future cryptographic standards.

features.title: Flexible deployment options
features.description: Available as open-source software, a software appliance, a hardware appliance, or a cloud edition on AWS and Azure marketplaces.

features.title: Audit logging and authentication controls
features.description: Every signing request can require authentication and produces a detailed audit log for compliance and forensic review.

pros: Mature product with roughly two decades of development history
Backed by Keyfactor, an established PKI and digital trust vendor
Free, open-source Community Edition available for evaluation or self-managed use
Broad support for signature formats: code, documents, containers, firmware, and timestamping
Flexible deployment as software, hardware appliance, or cloud marketplace instance
cons: Enterprise pricing is not published and requires contacting sales
Requires PKI and HSM expertise to configure and operate correctly
Not designed for individual e-signature use cases like contracts between people
Community Edition lacks the support SLAs and high-availability features of Enterprise
Cloud marketplace pricing (per-core, per-support-tier) can be complex to estimate upfront

faqs.question: What is SignServer used for?
faqs.answer: SignServer is used to automate digital signing of code, documents, containers, firmware, and other digital artifacts, along with RFC 3161 timestamping, typically as part of CI/CD pipelines or document-generation systems.

faqs.question: Is SignServer the same as an e-signature tool like SignNow or SignWell?
faqs.answer: No, SignServer is infrastructure software for automated, server-side cryptographic signing, while tools like SignNow, SignRequest, and SignWell are consumer e-signature platforms for individuals signing contracts by hand.

faqs.question: Who owns SignServer?
faqs.answer: SignServer was created by PrimeKey Solutions, which was acquired by Keyfactor in June 2021. Keyfactor now sponsors and sells SignServer.

faqs.question: Is SignServer free?
faqs.answer: The SignServer Community Edition is free and open-source under the LGPL v2.1 license. The Enterprise Edition is a paid, supported product with custom pricing.

faqs.question: How is SignServer Enterprise priced?
faqs.answer: Enterprise and cloud marketplace pricing is based on the number of CPU cores of the host and the chosen support tier; exact pricing requires contacting Keyfactor sales.

faqs.question: Does SignServer support Hardware Security Modules?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignServer is commonly deployed with HSMs to centrally protect private signing keys used for signing operations.

faqs.question: Can SignServer be used in CI/CD pipelines?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignServer is designed to integrate into CI/CD pipelines so builds and releases can be signed automatically without manual intervention.

faqs.question: When was SignServer first released?
faqs.answer: SignServer was first released in 2005 by PrimeKey Solutions; the Enterprise edition launched in 2012.

use_cases.title: CI/CD code signing
use_cases.description: DevOps teams integrate SignServer into build pipelines to automatically sign software releases, containers, and packages before distribution.

use_cases.title: Regulated document signing
use_cases.description: Organizations in finance, government, and healthcare use SignServer to apply verifiable digital signatures to PDFs and XML documents at scale.

use_cases.title: Trusted timestamping for compliance
use_cases.description: Companies requiring long-term proof of when an artifact was signed use SignServer's RFC 3161 timestamping to satisfy audit and compliance requirements.

enrichment.short_description: Simple, affordable e-signature software (formerly Docsketch) for sending, signing and tracking legally binding documents.
enrichment.seo_title: SignWell Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SignWell (formerly Docsketch) is an affordable e-signature platform. Compare its Free, Light, Business and Enterprise plans, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SignWell is an electronic signature platform, formerly branded Docsketch, that helps individuals and businesses send, sign, and track legally binding documents online. The product launched under the Docsketch name in 2019 and rebranded to SignWell in 2021 as it expanded from a document-drafting tool into a full e-signature service; the company is headquartered in Portland, Oregon, United States. SignWell markets itself as a simpler, more affordable alternative to larger e-signature platforms like DocuSign and Adobe Sign, with a pricing structure built around a free tier and low monthly per-sender pricing rather than complex enterprise contracts. The Free plan supports one sender, one template, and up to three documents per month at no cost; the Light plan starts at 10 US dollars per month (or 12 US dollars per month billed monthly with an annual discount) and removes the document cap while adding template links, bulk sending, conditional fields, and data validation; the Business plan starts at 30 US dollars per month and adds custom branding, request attachments, and in-person signing for up to three senders; and an Enterprise plan offers custom pricing for large teams with unlimited templates and documents and dedicated support. SignWell also offers a standalone API with pay-as-you-go pricing, starting at 0.85 US dollars per document after 25 free API documents per month, decreasing at higher volume. All plans include unlimited document signers at no extra cost, audit trails, and tamper-proof document sealing, and there are no long-term contracts or cancellation fees on any plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is SignWell
paragraphs: SignWell is an electronic signature platform that lets businesses and individuals send documents for signature, track their status, and store completed, legally binding agreements. It launched in 2019 under the name Docsketch and rebranded to SignWell in 2021 as the product grew from a simple document tool into a full e-signature service.

The company is headquartered in Portland, Oregon, and positions SignWell as a simpler, lower-cost alternative to legacy e-signature vendors such as DocuSign and Adobe Sign, targeting small businesses, freelancers, and mid-sized teams.

SignWell emphasizes transparent, predictable pricing with no long-term contracts or cancellation fees, along with unlimited signers on every plan, meaning only the sending party pays regardless of how many people need to sign a document.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of SignWell
paragraphs: SignWell provides reusable templates, template links that can be shared publicly for repeat use, and bulk sending to send the same document to many recipients at once.

The platform supports conditional fields and data validation within documents, custom branding on the signing experience for paid plans, and in-person signing for situations where signer and sender are in the same room.

Every plan includes an audit trail and tamper-proof document sealing to verify authenticity, and a standalone e-signature API is available for developers who want to embed signing directly into their own applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: SignWell Pricing
paragraphs: The Free plan supports one sender, one template, and up to three documents per month, making it suitable for occasional personal use.

The Light plan starts at 10 US dollars per month (12 US dollars per month if billed monthly versus a lower effective rate annually) and removes the document cap, adding five templates per sender, bulk send, and conditional fields; the Business plan starts at 30 US dollars per month for up to three senders and adds custom branding and in-person signing.

An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with unlimited templates, documents, and senders plus dedicated support, and the SignWell API is billed separately on a pay-as-you-go basis starting at 0.85 US dollars per document after the first 25 free API documents each month, with volume discounts down to about 0.20 US dollars per document.

features.title: Reusable templates and template links
features.description: Save frequently sent documents as templates, including shareable template links that let anyone request a signature without the sender manually initiating each send.

features.title: Bulk sending
features.description: Send the same document to a large group of recipients at once, useful for forms or agreements that many people need to sign individually.

features.title: Conditional fields and data validation
features.description: Build documents with fields that show, hide, or validate data based on signer input, reducing errors in forms and agreements.

features.title: Custom branding
features.description: Add company logos and branded colors to the signing experience on paid plans to keep the process consistent with your brand.

features.title: In-person signing
features.description: Collect signatures on a shared device when sender and signer are together, without needing to email the document.

features.title: Audit trail and tamper-proof sealing
features.description: Every completed document includes a detailed audit trail and cryptographic sealing to prove it has not been altered after signing.

features.title: Unlimited free signers
features.description: Only the sending account is billed; recipients can review and sign documents for free with no account required.

features.title: E-signature API
features.description: Developers can embed SignWell's signing workflow directly into their own applications with a pay-as-you-go API.

pros: Genuinely free tier available for occasional signers, not just a trial
Transparent, low-cost pricing compared to DocuSign and Adobe Sign
No long-term contracts or cancellation fees on any plan
Unlimited signers at no extra cost on every plan
Simple, clean interface that is quick to learn
cons: Lower-tier plans limit the number of senders and templates
Fewer advanced contract-lifecycle and workflow features than enterprise-focused competitors
API pricing can add up quickly for high-volume document senders
Company size and funding details are not widely publicly disclosed
Brand recognition is lower than established players like DocuSign, which can matter for enterprise buyers

faqs.question: Is SignWell the same company as Docsketch?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignWell was originally launched in 2019 as Docsketch and rebranded to SignWell in 2021 as the product expanded into a full e-signature service.

faqs.question: Does SignWell have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignWell's Free plan supports one sender, one template, and up to three documents per month at no cost.

faqs.question: How much does SignWell cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 10 US dollars per month for the Light plan, with the Business plan starting at 30 US dollars per month; an Enterprise plan offers custom pricing for larger teams.

faqs.question: Does SignWell charge document signers?
faqs.answer: No, document signers are always free and unlimited on every SignWell plan; only the sending account is billed.

faqs.question: Is SignWell legally binding?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignWell documents include an audit trail and tamper-proof sealing designed to meet legal requirements for electronic signatures.

faqs.question: Does SignWell offer an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignWell offers a standalone e-signature API with 25 free documents per month and pay-as-you-go pricing starting at 0.85 US dollars per document beyond that.

faqs.question: Where is SignWell based?
faqs.answer: SignWell is headquartered in Portland, Oregon, United States.

faqs.question: Are there cancellation fees for SignWell plans?
faqs.answer: No, SignWell states there are no contracts or cancellation fees, and monthly plans can be canceled at any time.

use_cases.title: Small business agreements
use_cases.description: Freelancers and small businesses use SignWell to send contracts, invoices, and proposals for e-signature without paying for enterprise features they don't need.

use_cases.title: Embedded signing in a product
use_cases.description: Software companies use the SignWell API to add e-signature capability directly inside their own application's document workflows.

use_cases.title: In-person and remote hybrid signing
use_cases.description: Teams that need both remote e-signature and occasional in-person signing use SignWell's shared in-person signing mode alongside standard email sending.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, no-code website builder for creating static sites visually, maintained by the non-profit Silex Labs.
enrichment.seo_title: Silex Review 2026: Free Open-Source No-Code Website Builder
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Silex is a free, open-source, no-code website builder for static sites. See features, hosting options, pros, cons and how it compares to Webflow.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Silex is a free and open-source, no-code website builder that lets designers and developers visually create static websites directly in the browser, positioned as an open alternative to proprietary builders like Webflow. The project traces back to 2003, created by Alex Hoyau along with Pol Goasdoue and Pierre Teissiere, and was substantially rebuilt from scratch in 2013 using JavaScript and Node.js to modernize the codebase. Silex is owned and maintained by Silex Labs, a French non-profit organization with no investors and no exit strategy, and it is a recognized project of OW2, the European open-source software community, having won the OW2 Best Project Award in the Technology category. Silex is licensed under the AGPL and is explicitly and permanently free: there are no paywalls, tiered feature restrictions, or premium-only functionality gating core use of the tool. Users can start building immediately in the hosted editor at v3.silex.me with nothing to install, or self-host the open-source server via Docker, Node.js, or a one-click deployment on CapRover for full control over their data and hosting. Silex supports drag-and-drop visual page building alongside direct HTML, CSS, and JavaScript editing for more advanced users, and it can pull dynamic content from external sources including WordPress, Strapi, Squidex, Supabase, or any GraphQL API, letting designers build headless, CMS-driven static sites without writing a backend. Optional paid services such as managed hosting or professional consulting are available from the community, but the core software itself remains free for everyone.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Silex
paragraphs: Silex is a free, open-source, no-code website builder for creating static websites visually in the browser. Its origins trace back to 2003, when it was created by Alex Hoyau together with Pol Goasdoue and Pierre Teissiere, and the tool was rebuilt from the ground up in 2013 with modern JavaScript and Node.js technology.

Silex is owned by Silex Labs, a French non-profit foundation with no investors and no exit strategy, which keeps the software permanently free and licensed under the AGPL. It is also recognized as a project of OW2, the European open-source community, having received the OW2 Best Project Award.

The tool is aimed at web designers, freelancers, and no-code builders who want a Webflow-style visual editing experience while keeping full ownership of the underlying static HTML, CSS, and JavaScript output, with the option to self-host rather than depend on a proprietary hosted platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of Silex
paragraphs: Silex offers a drag-and-drop visual editor for building responsive pages, alongside direct access to the underlying HTML, CSS, and JavaScript for designers who want finer control than pure no-code tools allow.

The platform can connect to external data sources including WordPress, Strapi, Squidex, Supabase, and any GraphQL API, letting users build dynamic, CMS-powered pages that are still exported as fast, static HTML.

Silex can be used instantly at v3.silex.me with nothing to install, or self-hosted via Docker, Node.js, or a one-click CapRover deployment, giving users full control over where their site's data and build process live.

long_form_blocks.heading: Silex Pricing
paragraphs: Silex is completely free with no paywalls: the project states explicitly that all features are free of charge and that the software will remain 100 percent free and open source for everyone.

Because Silex Labs is funded as a non-profit, including support from the NGI0 Commons Fund, rather than through investor capital, there is no commercial pressure to introduce paid tiers or restrict features behind a subscription.

Optional paid services exist around the free core product, such as managed hosting or professional consulting from community members and agencies, but using and self-hosting the Silex software itself never requires payment.

features.title: Drag-and-drop visual editor
features.description: Build responsive web pages visually in the browser without writing code, similar in spirit to commercial no-code builders.

features.description: Advanced users can edit the underlying code directly for fine-grained control beyond what the visual editor exposes.

features.description: Pulls dynamic content from WordPress, Strapi, Squidex, Supabase, or any GraphQL API to power data-driven static pages.

features.title: No installation required
features.description: Can be used immediately in the browser at v3.silex.me with no setup or download needed to start building.

features.title: Self-hosting options
features.description: Can be self-hosted via Docker, Node.js, or a one-click CapRover deployment for full control over data and infrastructure.

features.title: AGPL open-source license
features.description: Fully open-source under the AGPL, with the source code available on GitHub for inspection, modification, or contribution.

features.title: Non-profit governance
features.description: Owned and maintained by Silex Labs, a French non-profit with no investors, reducing the risk of future paywalls or feature lock-in.

pros: Completely free and open source with no feature paywalls
Can be self-hosted for full data ownership and control
Outputs clean, fast static HTML that is favorable for SEO
No investor pressure to monetize aggressively or introduce lock-in
Supports headless CMS integrations for dynamic content in a static site
cons: Smaller community and ecosystem compared to commercial builders like Webflow
Fewer built-in templates and pre-made design assets than paid competitors
Self-hosting requires basic technical knowledge of Docker or Node.js
Support relies on community and documentation rather than a dedicated commercial support team
Fewer native e-commerce and marketing integrations than mainstream no-code platforms

faqs.question: Is Silex really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Silex is completely free and open source with no paywalls; the project states all features are free of charge for everyone.

faqs.question: Who maintains Silex?
faqs.answer: Silex is maintained by Silex Labs, a French non-profit organization that owns the copyright and has no investors or exit strategy.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Silex?
faqs.answer: Yes, Silex can be self-hosted via Docker, Node.js, or a one-click deployment on CapRover, in addition to using the hosted editor at v3.silex.me.

faqs.question: Does Silex require coding knowledge?
faqs.answer: No, Silex is designed as a no-code, drag-and-drop visual builder, though it also allows direct HTML, CSS, and JavaScript editing for users who want more control.

faqs.question: Does Silex support dynamic content?
faqs.answer: Yes, Silex can connect to WordPress, Strapi, Squidex, Supabase, or any GraphQL API to pull in dynamic data while still outputting static HTML.

faqs.question: What license does Silex use?
faqs.answer: Silex is licensed under the AGPL (Affero General Public License), and its source code is publicly available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Is Silex good for SEO?
faqs.answer: Yes, because Silex outputs static HTML sites, pages tend to load quickly and are straightforward for search engines to crawl and index.

faqs.question: How is Silex funded if it's free?
faqs.answer: Silex Labs is a non-profit funded through grants such as the NGI0 Commons Fund and optional paid services like hosting or consulting, rather than through venture investment or software licensing fees.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted personal knowledge management app built on Markdown with an integrated Lua scripting environment.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free / open source (optional paid SilverBullet+ desktop edition)
enrichment.seo_title: SilverBullet Review 2026: Free Self-Hosted Notes App
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SilverBullet is a free, open-source, self-hosted Markdown notes app with Lua scripting and wiki-style linking. See features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SilverBullet is a free, open-source, self-hosted personal knowledge management application built around plain Markdown files, created by developer Zef Hemel and first released in 2022. It is described by its creator as a programmable, private, browser-based personal knowledge platform, and it distributes as a single self-contained server binary or Docker container that users install on their own infrastructure, such as a home server, VPS, or Raspberry Pi. All notes, referred to as a 'Space,' are stored as plain Markdown files on disk, giving users full ownership and portability of their data with no proprietary file format or cloud dependency. SilverBullet combines a clean live-preview Markdown editor with wiki-style bidirectional linking between pages using double-bracket page links and linked mentions, a built-in structured database and query language for treating notes as queryable data, and a fully integrated Lua scripting environment that lets users write custom commands, page templates, and interactive widgets directly inside their notes. The application runs as a Progressive Web App, supporting both online, synced, and fully offline operation once installed. SilverBullet is licensed under the MIT license, making it free and open source with no cost for the core self-hosted application; a separate commercial offering called SilverBullet+ exists for users who want a packaged desktop edition, but the underlying open-source project remains free. It is best suited to technically comfortable users, developers, and privacy-conscious note-takers who prefer self-hosting and plain-text data ownership over hosted SaaS note apps like Notion or Obsidian Sync.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is SilverBullet
paragraphs: SilverBullet is a free, open-source, self-hosted personal knowledge management application built on plain Markdown files. It was created by developer Zef Hemel and first released in 2022, and is described as a programmable, private, browser-based personal knowledge database.

Rather than storing notes in a proprietary format on someone else's servers, SilverBullet keeps a user's entire note collection, called a 'Space,' as plain Markdown files on disk on a server the user controls, distributed as a single self-contained binary or Docker container.
The tool is positioned for technically comfortable users who value data ownership, self-hosting, and scriptability over the convenience of a fully managed SaaS note app such as Notion.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of SilverBullet
paragraphs: SilverBullet provides a live-preview Markdown editor combined with wiki-style bidirectional linking, using double-bracket page links and automatic linked mentions to surface where a page is referenced elsewhere.
A built-in database and query language lets users treat their notes as structured, queryable data, enabling dashboards, task lists, and dynamic views built directly from Markdown content.
SilverBullet integrates a full Lua scripting environment that runs in the browser, allowing users to write custom commands, page templates, and interactive widgets to extend the editor's behavior.

long_form_blocks.heading: SilverBullet Pricing
paragraphs: SilverBullet's core application is free and open source under the MIT license, with no cost to download, self-host, or use indefinitely.
Because it is self-hosted, users are responsible for their own hosting costs, such as a VPS, home server, or Raspberry Pi, rather than paying a subscription to the SilverBullet project.

A separate commercial product, SilverBullet+, offers a packaged desktop edition for users who prefer a paid, pre-built experience, though the underlying open-source SilverBullet project remains free for anyone who wants to self-host it.

features.title: Plain Markdown storage
features.description: All notes are stored as plain Markdown files on disk, ensuring full data ownership and portability with no proprietary format.

features.title: Wiki-style bidirectional linking
features.description: Link pages together using double-bracket syntax and see automatic linked mentions showing where a page is referenced elsewhere.

features.title: Built-in query language
features.description: Query notes as structured data to build dynamic lists, dashboards, and views generated directly from Markdown content.

features.title: Lua scripting environment
features.description: Write custom commands, templates, and interactive widgets using an integrated Lua scripting engine that runs in the browser.

features.title: Offline-capable PWA
features.description: Runs as a Progressive Web App that supports fully offline operation once installed, syncing changes when connectivity returns.

features.title: Single-binary self-hosting
features.description: Deploys as a single self-contained server binary or Docker container, simplifying installation on a home server or VPS.

features.title: Live-preview editor
features.description: Edits Markdown with an immediate, clean live preview rather than requiring a separate render step.

features.title: Open-source and self-hosted
features.description: MIT-licensed and fully self-hosted, giving users complete control over their data with no vendor lock-in.

pros: Completely free and open source under the MIT license
Notes stored as plain, portable Markdown files with no lock-in
Powerful Lua scripting for users who want to customize their workflow
Works fully offline as a Progressive Web App
Strong privacy since data never leaves servers the user controls
cons: Requires self-hosting, which involves technical setup most non-technical users will find difficult
No built-in cloud sync between multiple self-hosted instances without additional configuration
Smaller community and plugin ecosystem than established tools like Obsidian
Lua scripting has a learning curve for users without a programming background
No official mobile apps; access is via the browser-based PWA

faqs.question: Is SilverBullet free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core SilverBullet application is free and open source under the MIT license. A separate paid SilverBullet+ desktop edition also exists for users who want a packaged product.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host SilverBullet?
faqs.answer: Yes, SilverBullet is a self-hosted application; you install it on your own server, VPS, or device such as a Raspberry Pi rather than using a hosted SaaS version.

faqs.question: Who created SilverBullet?
faqs.answer: SilverBullet was created by developer Zef Hemel and was first released in 2022.

faqs.question: What format are notes stored in?
faqs.answer: Notes are stored as plain Markdown files on disk in what SilverBullet calls a 'Space,' giving users full ownership and portability of their data.

faqs.question: Does SilverBullet support scripting?
faqs.answer: Yes, SilverBullet includes a fully integrated Lua scripting environment for writing custom commands, templates, and widgets.

faqs.question: Can SilverBullet be used offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, SilverBullet runs as a Progressive Web App and supports fully offline operation once installed, syncing when a connection is available.

faqs.question: Does SilverBullet support linking between notes?
faqs.answer: Yes, SilverBullet supports wiki-style bidirectional linking using double-bracket page links and shows linked mentions automatically.

faqs.question: How does SilverBullet compare to Obsidian?
faqs.answer: Both are Markdown-based knowledge tools, but SilverBullet is server-based and self-hosted with browser access and Lua scripting, while Obsidian is primarily a local desktop and mobile app with a plugin-based JavaScript ecosystem.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused personal knowledge base
use_cases.description: Individuals who want full control over their notes self-host SilverBullet to keep all data on their own infrastructure instead of a third-party cloud service.

use_cases.title: Developer-built custom workflows
use_cases.description: Developers use SilverBullet's Lua scripting environment to build custom commands, templates, and widgets tailored to their own note-taking workflow.

use_cases.title: Offline-first note-taking
use_cases.description: Users who need reliable offline access rely on SilverBullet's Progressive Web App mode to keep working without an internet connection.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source PHP content management system and framework from New Zealand, offering flexible content modeling and headless delivery.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free / open source (commercial support, hosting and development available)
enrichment.seo_title: Silverstripe CMS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Silverstripe CMS is an open-source PHP content management system from New Zealand. See features, pricing, pros, cons and top use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Silverstripe CMS is an open-source PHP content management system and framework originally founded as Totally Digital in Wellington, New Zealand, in February 2000 by Sam Minnee, Tim Copeland, and Sigurd Magnusson, all three of whom remain involved with the company today. The team built its first CMS version by 2003 and later open-sourced the platform, which significantly expanded its international adoption and use in large-scale government and enterprise projects. Silverstripe Ltd is a Wellington-based company with a team of around 100 developers, designers, and support staff, and it continues to lead ongoing development of the open-source Silverstripe CMS alongside a global open-source community. Notable organizations that have used Silverstripe CMS include Television New Zealand (TVNZ), the New Zealand Government, Whittaker's, Air New Zealand, and OpenStack. Silverstripe CMS is free and open source, distributed and maintained by the community, with no license fee to download, self-host, or modify the software; the Silverstripe company generates revenue through commercial support, hosting, and custom development services rather than software licensing. Technically, it combines a traditional content management system with an API-first, headless-capable architecture, allowing content to be delivered to websites as well as external applications. Core CMS features include flexible content blocks for page layout, granular user roles and permissions with approval workflows, version history with the ability to revert changes, and a large add-on and module ecosystem for extending functionality. Silverstripe is best suited to organizations, agencies, and government bodies that need a customizable, developer-friendly CMS without recurring software license costs, and that value having full source-code access and self-hosting control.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Silverstripe CMS
paragraphs: Silverstripe CMS is an open-source PHP content management system and framework that traces back to a company originally named Totally Digital, co-founded in Wellington, New Zealand, in February 2000 by Sam Minnee, Tim Copeland, and Sigurd Magnusson.

The founders built their first CMS product by 2003 in a Wellington tech incubator, and the decision to release the platform as open source became a turning point that expanded Silverstripe's international presence and scale of projects, including government and enterprise deployments.

Today, Silverstripe Ltd remains a Wellington-based company with roughly 100 developers, designers, and support staff, continuing to lead development of the open-source Silverstripe CMS alongside a global community of contributors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of Silverstripe CMS
paragraphs: Silverstripe CMS uses flexible content blocks that let editors compose page layouts without needing a developer for every content change, alongside built-in version control and history so any edit can be reverted.
The platform supports granular user roles, permissions, and content approval workflows, making it suitable for organizations that need editorial oversight before content is published.

Silverstripe is API-first and supports decoupled or headless delivery, meaning content managed in the CMS can also power external applications, mobile apps, or other frontends beyond the traditional website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Silverstripe CMS Pricing
paragraphs: Silverstripe CMS itself is free and open source, with no license fee required to download, install, or modify the software for any organization.
Silverstripe Ltd, the founding company, generates revenue through commercial support contracts, managed hosting, and custom development services rather than charging for the software license itself.
Organizations can also engage the wider Silverstripe partner network of agencies for implementation and ongoing development, with costs varying by project scope rather than a fixed software price.

features.title: Flexible content blocks
features.description: Editors compose page layouts from modular content blocks without needing developer intervention for routine content changes.

features.title: Version history and rollback
features.description: Every content change is tracked, allowing editors to view history and revert to a previous version if needed.

features.title: User roles and approval workflows
features.description: Granular permissions and content approval workflows support organizations that require editorial review before publishing.

features.title: Headless and decoupled delivery
features.description: An API-first architecture lets content power external applications and additional frontends beyond the main website.

features.title: Built-in security features
features.description: Includes security tooling designed to protect content and data, relevant for government and enterprise deployments.

features.title: Large add-on ecosystem
features.description: Thousands of community modules and extensions are available to add functionality without custom development.

features.title: Open-source flexibility
features.description: Full source code access lets development teams customize the CMS to fit specific technical or compliance requirements.

features.title: Enterprise and government track record
features.description: Used by organizations such as TVNZ, the New Zealand Government, Whittaker's, Air New Zealand, and OpenStack for large-scale deployments.

pros: Free and open source with no software licensing cost
Over two decades of active development and a mature codebase
Strong track record with government and large enterprise deployments
API-first architecture supports headless and multi-channel content delivery
Large ecosystem of modules and an established partner network
cons: Requires PHP development expertise to customize beyond out-of-the-box functionality
Smaller global market share and community than WordPress or Drupal
Self-hosting means the organization is responsible for infrastructure, updates, and security patching unless using a managed partner
Learning curve for content editors coming from simpler page builders
Commercial support and custom development costs vary and are not fixed like a SaaS subscription

faqs.question: Is Silverstripe CMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Silverstripe CMS is free and open source with no license fee; the founding company monetizes through commercial support, hosting, and development services instead.

faqs.question: Where was Silverstripe CMS founded?
faqs.answer: Silverstripe was founded in Wellington, New Zealand, in February 2000, originally under the name Totally Digital.

faqs.question: Who founded Silverstripe?
faqs.answer: Silverstripe was co-founded by Sam Minnee, Tim Copeland, and Sigurd Magnusson, all of whom remain involved with the company today.

faqs.question: Is Silverstripe CMS headless?
faqs.answer: Silverstripe CMS supports both traditional and headless or decoupled delivery, with an API-first architecture that can power external applications as well as a standard website.

faqs.question: Who uses Silverstripe CMS?
faqs.answer: Notable organizations that have used Silverstripe CMS include TVNZ, the New Zealand Government, Whittaker's, Air New Zealand, and OpenStack.

faqs.question: Does Silverstripe require a developer to use?
faqs.answer: Content editors can manage pages and content blocks without coding, but customizing themes, modules, or advanced functionality typically requires PHP development skills.

faqs.question: How is Silverstripe different from WordPress?
faqs.answer: Silverstripe is a PHP framework-based CMS with a strong emphasis on structured content, permissions, and headless delivery, often chosen for government and enterprise projects, while WordPress has a larger plugin ecosystem and market share overall.

faqs.question: How big is the company behind Silverstripe?
faqs.answer: Silverstripe Ltd is a Wellington, New Zealand-based company with a team of roughly 100 developers, designers, and support staff.

use_cases.title: Government and public sector websites
use_cases.description: Government agencies use Silverstripe CMS for its security features, approval workflows, and track record on public sector projects.

use_cases.title: Enterprise multi-channel content delivery
use_cases.description: Large organizations use Silverstripe's API-first, headless capabilities to deliver the same content to a website, mobile app, and other digital channels.

use_cases.title: Agency-built custom websites
use_cases.description: Web development agencies use Silverstripe's flexible framework to build custom, developer-controlled websites for clients that need more than a template-based builder.

features: Full CMS and framework source code

enrichment.short_description: Publicly traded (NYSE: SMWB) digital intelligence platform providing web traffic analytics, market research and competitive benchmarking.
enrichment.seo_title: Similarweb Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Similarweb (NYSE: SMWB) is a digital intelligence platform for web traffic and market analytics. Compare pricing plans, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Similarweb is a publicly traded digital intelligence and web analytics platform, listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker SMWB since its IPO in May 2021 at a roughly 1.6 billion US dollar valuation. The company was founded in 2007 and is headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, with additional offices across roughly 15 global locations and a workforce estimated between 1,000 and 5,000 employees. Similarweb's platform estimates website and app traffic, audience demographics, engagement metrics, referral and keyword data, and competitive benchmarking across more than 100 million websites and apps, serving over 10,000 paying customers spanning marketing, sales, investment research, and e-commerce teams. Pricing is structured across self-service and sales-quoted tiers: the self-service Starter plan starts around 125 US dollars per month when billed annually (or about 149 US dollars per month billed monthly), and a Professional self-service plan runs roughly 333 to 399 US dollars per month depending on billing cycle. Higher tiers such as Team and Business are sold through Similarweb's sales team with published estimates around 14,000 and 35,000 US dollars per year respectively for a handful of users and defined history and keyword limits, while Enterprise plans are fully custom, typically requiring a minimum of 10 users, offering the longest historical data window, unlimited keyword tracking, and API access, with real-world enterprise contract values commonly ranging from tens of thousands to over 100,000 US dollars annually depending on scope. Similarweb is commonly used by SEO teams, competitive intelligence analysts, investors, and e-commerce and marketing teams that need third-party estimates of a website's or app's traffic and audience rather than first-party analytics from tools like Google Analytics.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Similarweb
paragraphs: Similarweb is a digital intelligence and web analytics platform founded in 2007 and headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel. The company went public on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker SMWB in May 2021 at a valuation of approximately 1.6 billion US dollars.

With operations spanning roughly 15 global locations and an employee base estimated between 1,000 and 5,000 people, Similarweb has grown into one of the most widely cited sources for third-party website and app traffic estimates.

The platform serves over 10,000 paying customers, including marketing teams, SEO professionals, investors, and competitive intelligence analysts who use Similarweb to estimate traffic, audience behavior, and market share for websites and apps they do not own or have direct analytics access to.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of Similarweb
paragraphs: Similarweb estimates traffic and engagement for websites and mobile apps, including visits, time on site, bounce rate, page views, and audience demographics, even for sites the user does not control.

The platform provides competitive benchmarking, showing how a website's traffic, channel mix, and keywords compare against direct competitors, along with market and industry-level analysis for broader trend research.

Additional modules cover keyword research, referral and backlink analysis, app intelligence for mobile app rankings and usage, and investment-grade market research used by financial analysts and investors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Similarweb Pricing
paragraphs: Similarweb's self-service Starter plan begins around 125 US dollars per month when billed annually, or about 149 US dollars per month on monthly billing, aimed at individuals and small teams needing basic traffic and competitive data.

A self-service Professional plan runs roughly 333 US dollars per month billed annually (around 399 US dollars per month billed monthly), adding expanded historical data, keyword limits, and additional modules.

Higher tiers, including Team, Business, and Enterprise plans, are sold through Similarweb's sales team with custom quotes based on number of users, months of historical data, keyword volume, and API access; published estimates place Team plans around 14,000 US dollars per year and Business plans around 35,000 US dollars per year, while Enterprise contracts, requiring a minimum of 10 users, commonly range from the tens of thousands to well over 100,000 US dollars annually.

features.title: Website traffic estimation
features.description: Estimates visits, engagement, and traffic trends for any website, including competitors' sites the user does not own.

features.title: Audience and demographic analysis
features.description: Provides estimated audience demographics, interests, and geographic distribution for a given website or app.

features.title: Competitive benchmarking
features.description: Compares a site's traffic, channel mix, and market share directly against named competitors.

features.title: Keyword and SEO research
features.description: Surfaces organic and paid keyword data, search volume, and ranking insights for SEO and PPC research.

features.title: App intelligence
features.description: Tracks mobile app download estimates, usage, and rankings across app stores.

features.title: Referral and traffic source analysis
features.description: Breaks down where a website's traffic comes from, including referral sites, social channels, and paid sources.

features.title: Market and industry research
features.description: Provides aggregated market-level data used by investors and strategists to analyze industry trends.

features.title: API access
features.description: Enterprise plans include API access for pulling Similarweb data directly into internal tools and dashboards.

pros: Large, well-established dataset covering traffic estimates for millions of websites and apps
Useful for competitive research on sites you don't have direct analytics access to
Publicly traded company (NYSE: SMWB) with established financial transparency
Broad feature set spanning SEO, market research, and app intelligence in one platform
Trusted by investors and analysts as a third-party data source
cons: Higher-tier plans require sales contact rather than transparent self-service pricing
Traffic and audience figures are estimates, not exact first-party analytics
Enterprise pricing can run into the tens or hundreds of thousands of dollars annually
Self-service Starter and Professional plans have limited historical data and keyword allowances
Smaller or lower-traffic websites may have less accurate estimated data than high-traffic sites

faqs.question: Is Similarweb publicly traded?
faqs.answer: Yes, Similarweb trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker SMWB, having gone public in May 2021 at a valuation of roughly 1.6 billion US dollars.

faqs.question: When was Similarweb founded?
faqs.answer: Similarweb was founded in 2007 and is headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel.

faqs.question: How much does Similarweb cost?
faqs.answer: Self-service plans start around 125 US dollars per month billed annually for the Starter tier, with Professional plans around 333 to 399 US dollars per month; Team, Business, and Enterprise tiers require a custom quote from sales.

faqs.question: What does Similarweb actually measure?
faqs.answer: Similarweb provides estimated website and app traffic, audience demographics, engagement, referral sources, and keyword data, generated from a mix of panel data, public data, and machine learning models rather than direct site analytics access.

faqs.question: Is Similarweb accurate?
faqs.answer: Similarweb data is an estimate rather than exact, and accuracy tends to be higher for high-traffic websites than for smaller or niche sites with limited data signals.

faqs.question: Who uses Similarweb?
faqs.answer: Similarweb serves over 10,000 paying customers, including SEO and marketing teams, e-commerce businesses, investors, and competitive intelligence analysts.

faqs.question: Does Similarweb offer an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, API access is included with Similarweb's Enterprise plan for organizations that want to pull data directly into their own systems.

faqs.question: How big is Similarweb as a company?
faqs.answer: Similarweb has an estimated 1,000 to 5,000 employees across roughly 15 global office locations.

use_cases.title: Competitive traffic benchmarking
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use Similarweb to compare their website's traffic, channels, and audience against named competitors.

use_cases.title: SEO and keyword research
use_cases.description: SEO professionals use Similarweb's keyword and search data to identify ranking opportunities and organic traffic trends.

use_cases.title: Investment and market research
use_cases.description: Investors and analysts use Similarweb's traffic and market data as an alternative data source to inform investment decisions about public and private companies.
